summary: I worked in the sliced bacon department at Swift & Company, and I used to think Swift's was the cream of the crop. They made it seem like we were one big happy family, but when layoffs started, it was clear they didn't care much. They wouldn't even hire a Negro girl in sliced bacon, though they had them doing dirty jobs elsewhere in the yards. My brother worked there too, and even though he was shot by a watchman during a strike in 1919, he never joined the union and still defends Swift like he owns it.

Wilson's was a real craphole to work at back in 1934. I was working 10 to 12 hours a day, and I was blacklisted because someone found out I was friendly to the union. They broke up my seniority and eventually stopped putting me back on the job. When I confronted the head employment manager, he basically told me I was done and to keep waiting in the employment office if I wanted. It was clear they wanted to keep control and keep us down.

The union has changed a lot of attitudes among the yard workers, even though companies like Swift tried to beat them to the punch by raising wages first. Many workers don't realize that without the union, those wage increases wouldn't have happened. The union also fights against the harsh working conditions and diseases like hog's itch that are common in the yards. I was friendly to the union, but that got me blacklisted at Wilson's. Still, the union is crucial for protecting us from the company's tactics.

Hog's itch is a nasty rash that a lot of yard workers get. It looks awful, is really irritating and sore, and it spreads easily. I know a guy who had to quit his job three years ago because of it, and the doctors still can't cure him. It's just one of the many occupational diseases that people in the yards suffer from, along with rheumatism and varicose veins. It shows how tough and unhealthy the work can be.

My brother worked in the hog kill for eight years, but when work started getting scarce, they began cutting into the seniority list, so he was lucky they transferred him to beef kill. Beef kill was part of the yards where he continued working after the hog kill. The yards are tough places with lots of health risks, but people like my brother stick it out. The beef kill was just another hard job in the yards where seniority and transfers mattered a lot.
